coredata.dc:description: The conversion of carbon dioxide (CO2) into value-added chemicals is a pressing necessity to address the challenges posed by global warming and glacier melting. The reverse water gas shift (RWGS) reaction is a promising chemical conversion method for transforming CO2 into value-added carbon monoxide (CO). However, current photocatalytic processes and thermal catalysis suffer from low efficiency in light energy utilization and necessitates high-temperature conditions, respectively, resulting in suboptimal CO2 conversion rates and increased energy consumption. Therefore, the development of catalysts that exhibit efficient photothermal effects is crucial for enhancing the catalytic performance of RWGS, as photothermal catalysis can compensate for the inefficiencies of photocatalysis and the energy demands of thermal catalysis. Herein, we successfully synthesized photothermal catalysts using Zr-MOF metal–organic frameworks encapsulating varying amounts of carbon quantum dots (CDs) loaded with highly dispersed platinum (Pt) nanoparticles (NPs) to improve RWGS reaction performance at low temperatures. Our results indicate that the incorporation of CDs within Zr-MOF enhances the range of light absorption and minimizes the recombination of photogenerated carriers. Furthermore, the presence of Pt NPs further mitigates carrier recombination, significantly improving carrier separation efficiency. Following optimization, the Pt/30-CDs/Zr-MOF catalyst (with a Pt loading of 1.0 wt%) demonstrated impressive low-temperature RWGS performance, achieving a CO2 conversion rate of 12.24 %, CO selectivity exceeding 99 %, and a CO yield of 22.52 mmol gcat -1h−1 under conditions of 300 °C, a light intensity of 1.2 W cm−2, and a gas-hourly space velocity of 24000 mL gcat -1h−1. This yield is nearly double that obtained under purely thermal conditions. High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy confirms that CDs are effectively encapsulated by Zr-MOF along with Pt NPs exhibiting a small particle size of 2.84 nm. Additionally, the photothermal and photovoltaic performance analyses indicate that the incorporation of a moderate amounts of CDs significantly mitigates the recombination of photogenerated carriers, thereby enhancing photocurrent and photogenerated heat while reducing impedance. Consequently, the improved photothermal catalytic performance of the Pt/30-CDs/Zr-MOF composite can be primarily attributed to the high dispersion of Pt NPs, the interaction between Pt and CDs that minimizes the recombination of photogenerated carriers, and a subsequent reduction in the activation energy required for the reaction. This study presents a viable approach for the synthesis of MOF-encapsulated CDs-loaded noble metal-based catalysts aimed at facilitating CO2 reduction at low temperatures.

head.ce:title: Encapsulating carbon quantum dots by Zr-MOF-supported Pt nanoparticles for enhanced photothermal RWGS reaction

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The synergistic interaction between platinum nanoparticles (NPs) and CDs effectively lowers the activation energy, thereby facilitating low-temperature photothermal reverse water–gas shift (RWGS) reaction. Furthermore, Pt/30-CDs/Zr/MOF demonstrates an smaller bandgap width with excellent photoelectric response, which minimizes the recombination of charge carriers generated by light. Under conditions of 300 °C and 1.2 W cm−2 illumination, the carbon dioxide conversion rate achieved was 12.24%, with a CO yield of 22.52 mmol gcat -1h−1, and CO selectivity exceeding 99.9%.

The conversion of carbon dioxide (CO2) into value-added chemicals is a pressing necessity to address the challenges posed by global warming and glacier melting. The reverse water gas shift (RWGS) reaction is a promising chemical conversion method for transforming CO2 into value-added carbon monoxide (CO). However, current photocatalytic processes and thermal catalysis suffer from low efficiency in light energy utilization and necessitates high-temperature conditions, respectively, resulting in suboptimal CO2 conversion rates and increased energy consumption. Therefore, the development of catalysts that exhibit efficient photothermal effects is crucial for enhancing the catalytic performance of RWGS, as photothermal catalysis can compensate for the inefficiencies of photocatalysis and the energy demands of thermal catalysis. Herein, we successfully synthesized photothermal catalysts using Zr-MOF metal–organic frameworks encapsulating varying amounts of carbon quantum dots (CDs) loaded with highly dispersed platinum (Pt) nanoparticles (NPs) to improve RWGS reaction performance at low temperatures. Our results indicate that the incorporation of CDs within Zr-MOF enhances the range of light absorption and minimizes the recombination of photogenerated carriers. Furthermore, the presence of Pt NPs further mitigates carrier recombination, significantly improving carrier separation efficiency. Following optimization, the Pt/30-CDs/Zr-MOF catalyst (with a Pt loading of 1.0 wt%) demonstrated impressive low-temperature RWGS performance, achieving a CO2 conversion rate of 12.24 %, CO selectivity exceeding 99 %, and a CO yield of 22.52 mmol gcat -1h−1 under conditions of 300 °C, a light intensity of 1.2 W cm−2, and a gas-hourly space velocity of 24000 mL gcat -1h−1. This yield is nearly double that obtained under purely thermal conditions. High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy confirms that CDs are effectively encapsulated by Zr-MOF along with Pt NPs exhibiting a small particle size of 2.84 nm. Additionally, the photothermal and photovoltaic performance analyses indicate that the incorporation of a moderate amounts of CDs significantly mitigates the recombination of photogenerated carriers, thereby enhancing photocurrent and photogenerated heat while reducing impedance. Consequently, the improved photothermal catalytic performance of the Pt/30-CDs/Zr-MOF composite can be primarily attributed to the high dispersion of Pt NPs, the interaction between Pt and CDs that minimizes the recombination of photogenerated carriers, and a subsequent reduction in the activation energy required for the reaction. This study presents a viable approach for the synthesis of MOF-encapsulated CDs-loaded noble metal-based catalysts aimed at facilitating CO2 reduction at low temperatures.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The significant increase in atmospheric carbon dioxide (CO2) concentrations has led to a range of serious environmental issues, including global warming, glacial melting, and ocean acidification. To mitigate these challenges, the conversion of CO2 into valuable fuels or chemical compounds is essential for achieving carbon recycling [1]. Currently, the primary methods for converting CO2 include hydrogenation to synthesize methanol, the Sabatier reaction to produce methane, methane dry reforming reaction, the reverse water–gas shift (RWGS) reaction, and CO2 hydrogenation to generate C2 hydrocarbons, alcohols, and other compounds [2,3]. Among these methods, the RWGS reaction is particularly promising for CO2 conversion, as it produces carbon monoxide (CO), which can subsequently be utilized to synthesize high-carbon compounds through the Fischer-Tropsch (F-T) reaction [4–6]. Since the RWGS reaction is endothermic (CO2 + H2 → CO + H2O, ΔH 298K = 41.2 kJ mol−1), elevated temperatures and substantial energy inputs are generally required to achieve high CO2 conversion and CO yield [7]. However, high reaction temperatures can lead to sintering and deactivation of the catalysts [8]. Therefore, developing RWGS catalysts that demonstrate high CO2 conversion and CO selectivity at lower temperatures, while maintaining good stability, remains a significant challenge.

In contrast to the high energy consumption associated with traditional thermal catalysis and the low light energy utilization found in photocatalysis, photothermal co-catalysis effectively integrates the distinct advantages of both approaches [9]. Among other things, the role of light is to oxidize surface carbon deposition precursors by photogenerated cavities as a way to solve the problem of carbon deposition caused by high temperatures in conventional thermal catalysis. On the other hand, the photoexcited electrons are directly involved in the reactant activation, and the heat accelerates the surface reaction with a lower apparent activation energy [10,11]. Consequently, the synergistic interplay of heat and light significantly enhances the efficiency of CO2 conversion and CO yield during catalytic reactions. Presently, the primary photothermal catalysts employed for the RWGS reaction include metal oxides [12], chalcogenides [13], and metal–organic framework (MOF) [14], etc. MOFs are particularly promising for applications in photothermal catalysis due to their well-defined crystal structures, high porosity, tunable chemical compositions, and abundant active sites [15]. For example, Jing et al. [16] have successfully incorporated silver nanoparticles (Ag NPs) into MIL-100 (Fe) through a straightforward solution impregnation-reduction method, leveraging the strong localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) effect of Ag NPs. The resulting Ag/MIL-100 (Fe) composites demonstrate efficient photothermal effects and catalytic capabilities. Despite the extensive research on MOFs in photothermal catalysis, they still encounter the challenge of low charge separation rates [17]. Recent investigations have revealed that carbon nanodots (CDs), a novel class of nanomaterials, exhibit exceptional light absorption properties and promote rapid electron transfer [18]. Currently, the strategy of employing CDs as electron acceptors on the surfaces of MOFs to enhance the photothermal performance of catalysts has been implemented [19]. For instance, Wang et al. [14] embedded CDs within zeolite ZIF-8, resulting in the formation of yellow CDs@ZIF-8 composites characterized by high structural stability. This stability arises from the electrostatic adsorption between the CDs and ZIF-8, which enhances the degree of electron cloud exclusion and, consequently, improves the photoluminescence quantum yield of the composites.

However, research on MOF-encapsulated CDs loaded with noble metal-based catalysts for photothermal co-catalysis of the RWGS reaction at low-temperatures remains limited. In this study, we designed Zr-MOF encapsulated with varying contents of CDs, which were loaded with highly dispersed Pt NPs as catalysts. The incorporation of Pt NPs after the encapsulation of CDs onto Zr-MOF significantly enhanced the catalyst's performance. Characterization results indicate that the synergistic interaction between CDs and Pt NPs promotes light absorption and utilization while mitigating the recombination of photogenerated carriers. Notably, the optimal sample, Pt/30-CDs/Zr-MOF, exhibited impressive performance in the photothermal catalytic RWGS reaction, achieving a CO2 conversion of 12.24 %, a selectivity exceeding 99 %, and a CO yield of approximately 22.52 mmol gcat -1h−1 under illumination conditions of 300 °C and 1.2 W cm−2. Furthermore, the Arrhenius fitting plot demonstrated that photothermal co-catalysis effectively reduced the activation energy of the RWGS reaction to 59.45 kJ mol−1, in contrast to pure thermal catalysis, which exhibited a significantly higher activation energy of 70.00 kJ mol−1.

All reagents can be used directly without further purification. Zirconium chloride (AR, 98 %), terephthalic acid (AR, 99 %), and trimethylamine solution (30 wt% in H2O) were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co. N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) (AR, 99.5 %), acetic acid (AR, 99.5 %), tetrachloroplatinic acid hexahydrate (H2PtCl4·6H2O) were purchased from Marell (Shanghai) Chemical Ltd. Glucose anhydrous was purchased from Tianjin Windship Chemical Reagent Technology.

Zr-MOF was synthesized via hydrothermal crystallization [20]. Initially, zirconium chloride (2.33 g) and 1,4-terephthalic acid (1.87 g) were dissolved in 60 mL of N,N-dimethylformamide after 30 min of ultrasonication, resulting in solution A. Subsequently, trimethylamine (5 μL) and acetic acid (7.5 mL) were sequentially added to solution A, which was stirred for 1 h to produce solution B. This mixed solution B was then transferred to a 100 mL autoclave and maintained at 120 °C for 24 h. The resulting Zr-MOF was collected, washed with ethanol five times, and dried at 60 °C for 12 h.

The G/Zr-MOF (where G represents glucose) was synthesized using a post-synthesis strategy. Specifically, 3 g of Zr-MOF was dissolved in various concentrations of glucose solution (100 mL) at 10, 20, 30, and 50 mM, with a solvent composition of ethanol and water in a 9:1 ratio, and stirred for 24 h. The resulting material was collected as G/Zr-MOF, washed five times with ethanol, and dried for 12 h at 60 °C.

The synthesis of CDs/Zr-MOF was achieved through mild pyrolysis. The G/Zr-MOF precursor was pyrolyzed at a rate of 3 °C min−1 until reaching 240 °C, followed by roasting under a nitrogen atmosphere for 4 h to yield (x)-CDs/Zr-MOF, where x denotes the glucose concentration.

Pt/x-CDs/Zr-MOF (x = 10, 20, 30, 50) were prepared using the impregnation method [21].The x-CDs/Zr-MOF support was dispersed in 10 mL of deionized water, to which a specific volume of H2PtCl4 solution was added. The mixture was stirred thoroughly and then heated to 60 °C, followed by drying in an oven at the same temperature for 12 h. The resulting solid powder was pressed into flakes, sieved into small particles (20 to 40 mesh), and subsequently placed in a reactor for 1 h at 300 °C under hydrogen reduction to produce the Pt/x-CDs/Zr-MOF catalyst, achieving a platinum loading of 1.0 wt%.

Lastly, the comparison sample, Pt/Zr-MOF, was synthesized using the impregnation method. The Zr-MOF support was dispersed in 10 mL of deionized water, followed by the addition of a specific amount of H2PtCl4 solution. The mixture was well-stirred and heated to 60 °C, then dried in an oven at the same temperature for 12 h. The resulting solid powder was pressed into flakes, sieved into small particles (20 to 40 mesh), and subjected to hydrogen reduction in a reactor for 1 h at 300 °C to obtain the Pt/Zr-MOF catalyst, also achieving a platinum loading of 1.0 wt%.

The morphological structure of the samples was observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) using a Hitachi SU8230 instrument at an operating voltage of 5 kV. The morphology was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), as well as energy spectrometry (EDS) on a JEM 2100F model at an operating voltage of 200 kV. The structure of the catalysts and the crystallization process were characterized by X-ray diffractometry (XRD).

The catalysts were analyzed by XRD using a SmartLab SE diffractometer under Cu Kα 40 kV, 30 mA radiation in the range of 5° ∼ 90° with the scanning speed set to 8°/min. The catalysts were analyzed using a Quantachrome Autosorb IQ instrument at −196 °C with nitrogen as the adsorption and desorption gas using a Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) and Brunauer-Emeth- teller (BET) method to calculate the specific surface area, pore volume, and average pore size of the samples, respectively. The electronic states of the surface elements and the photoelectric processes at a light intensity of 1.2 W cm−2 were analyzed by obtaining X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and in-situ X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (In-situ XPS) using a ThermoFischer ESCALAB 250Xi instrument. The electronic states of the elements C 1 s, Zr 3d, O 1 s, and Pt 4f were obtained and the binding energy was calibrated by the C 1 s electron peak (284.8 eV).

Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra of the samples were obtained at room temperature using a Bruker A300-9.5/12 instrument (Germany). Experiments on H2-programmed thermal reduction (H2-TPR) and CO2-programmed thermal desorption (CO2-TPD) were carried out using the BELCAT-A, a programmed temperature-raising chemisorbent analyzer. For CO2-TPD, the sample was pretreated with He gas at 120 °C for 1 h. Subsequently, the sample was cooled down to 50 °C. After saturated adsorption, the sample was heated up to 400 °C at 5 °C min−1, and the concentration of the desorbed CO2 was measured. The test process for CO-TPD was similar to that of CO2-TPD but with CO as adsorbed gas. The procedure for the H2-TPR measurement was as follows: the samples were first pretreated with Ar gas at 120 °C for 1 h. Subsequently, the samples were cooled to 50 °C. The samples were then heated to 400 °C with the addition of the H2/Ar mixture. The consumed H2 concentration is measured by adding the H2/Ar mixture and then warming up to 300 °C at 5 °C min−1.

Photoluminescence spectra (PL) were obtained using a Hitachi F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) in the range of 200 ∼ 800 nm were recorded using a Shimadzu UV-2500 spectrophotometer to obtain the properties of the materials in response to light. The photothermal conversion ability of the six catalysts was tested using a hand-held thermal imaging camera (DS-2TPH10-3AUF) under a 300 W xenon lamp with a light intensity of 1.2W cm−2. To account for the differences in reaction rates among various catalysts under light and dark conditions, we calculated the apparent activation energies for all samples using the Arrhenius formula. The reactions were conducted in a quartz glass tube fixed-bed reactor, which was heated to 300 °C under a nitrogen atmosphere at a flow rate of 20 mL min−1. The system was allowed to stabilize for 5 min before switching to a hydrogen atmosphere, where it was reduced at atmospheric pressure for 1 h. The reacting gas mixture, with a volumetric ratio of CO2:H2:N2 set at 1:4:2, was introduced into the reactor at a flow rate of 64 mL min−1, corresponding to a gas-hourly space velocity of 24000 mL gcat -1h−1. A 300 W Xenon lamp served as the light source, and the reaction conditions varied from 200 °C to 300 °C at atmospheric pressure.

Photocurrent, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and Mott-Schottky (M−S) were detected using an electrochemical workstation (CHI-760E). Silver/silver chloride (Ag/AgCl) and platinum sheets were used for the reference and counter electrodes, respectively, and the samples to be tested were uniformly coated on the FTO sheets (with an area of about 1 cm2) as working electrodes. The spare electrolyte was a 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution with a pH of about 7. The light source was a 300 W xenon lamp.

In-situ diffuse reflectance infrared fourier transform spectroscopy (DRIFTS) was tested on an INVENIO S, Bruker instrument. The catalyst was first heated to 50 °C under N2 purge. After stabilization, the catalyst was purged for 20 min, and the spectra were collected with the sample slice as the background. Pure CO2 was introduced at a gas flow rate of 20 mL min−1, and the spectra were collected at 200 °C, 225 °C, 250 °C, 275 °C and 300 °C, respectively. Then the temperature was lowered to 200 °C and the catalyst was purged with N2 for 60 min, switching to CO2/H2 mixture with a gas flow rate of 20 mL min−1 to collect spectra at 200 °C, 225 °C, 250 °C, 275 °C and 300 °C, respectively.

The photothermal catalytic performance of the catalysts was assessed using a quartz glass tube fixed bed reactor. Before the photothermal CO2 reduction reaction, the dried powder sample was pressed and sieved into catalyst particles measuring 20 to 40 mesh. A total of 0.16 g of the catalyst was combined with 0.52 g of silicon carbide (SiC) and subsequently loaded into the reactor. The catalyst was initially heated to 200 °C at a flow rate of 20 mL min−1 under a N2 atmosphere. Following this, the atmosphere was switched to H2, and the temperature was increased to 300 °C for reduction over a duration of 1 h at atmospheric pressure. Catalyst testing was conducted at temperatures ranging from 200 °C to 400 °C, also under atmospheric pressure. The temperature of the catalyst surface was monitored using a thermocouple. The reaction gas mixture was introduced into the reactor at a flow rate of 64 mL min−1, with a volume ratio of CO2:H2:N2 set at 1:4:2. The photothermal-catalyzed CO2 reduction reaction utilized a 300 W xenon lamp as the light source, delivering a light intensity of 1.2 W cm−2, while the gas-phase air velocity during the reaction was maintained at 24000 mL gcat −1h−1.The schematic of the experimental setup is shown in Fig. 1 . The gaseous products generated were directed from the reactor to an Agilent GC-8860 chromatograph equipped with a thermal conductivity detector (TCD) for analysis. At the conclusion of the reaction, the catalyst was allowed to cool naturally to room temperature under a nitrogen atmosphere. The calculations for CO2 conversion (X

In Equation (3), F represents the reaction gas flow rate (mL min−1), and [CO] is the concentration of CO (vol%) detected by the on-line gas chromatograph. In Equation (4), RCO represents the CO production rate, and the nCO is the result of equation (3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and morphological characteristics of catalysts
ce:section.ce:para:
In this study, a Zr-MOF precursor was synthesized through hydrothermal crystallization, followed by the in-situ loading of glucose (G) to yield G/Zr-MOF via a post-synthesis method. The G/Zr-MOF was subsequently subjected to mild pyrolysis treatment in a nitrogen atmosphere at 240 °C, resulting in the encapsulation of CDs within the microporous channels of the Zr-MOF. Pt nanoparticles were then supported on the CDs/Zr-MOF supports by using the impregnation method. This process involved dispersing the supports in 10 mL of deionized water, to which a specific volume of H2PtCl4 solution was added. The mixture was thoroughly stirred and heated to 60 °C, followed by drying at the same temperature for 12 h. The resulting solid powder was pressed into flakes and sieved to obtain small particles (20 ∼ 40 mesh), which were then placed into a reactor for reduction under a hydrogen atmosphere at 300 °C for 1 h, yielding the Pt/x-CDs/Zr-MOF catalysts (as illustrated in Fig. 2
a). The Pt/Zr-MOF and 30-CDs/Zr-MOF catalysts were prepared using analogous procedures, as detailed in the experimental section. The surface morphology of the catalysts was analyzed using SEM, as shown in Fig. S1 . The SEM images indicate that both Pt/x-CDs/Zr-MOF and 30-CDs/Zr-MOF exhibit irregularly shaped polyhedra, with the concentration of CDs and the loading of Pt not significantly influencing the morphological structure of the catalysts [22]. Additionally, TEM images of Pt/30-CDs/Zr-MOF at scales of 100 nm and 50 nm (shown in Fig. 2 b-2c) reveal the presence of Pt nanoparticles. Similar observations were made in the TEM images of Pt/x-CDs/Zr-MOF (where x = 0, 10, 20, 50) presented in Figs. S2-S5. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images (Fig. 2 d and Figs. S2-S5) reveal a crystallite spacing of 0.22 nm, which corresponds to the (111) plane of Pt, thereby confirming the successful loading of Pt nanoparticles. The interface contact between Pt and CDs was also found by analyzing HR-TEM (Fig. S7). There was a close contact interface between the Pt (111) and CDs (100) as the crystal plane corresponding to the 0.22 nm of Pt and the crystal plane corresponding to the 0.25 nm of CDs. Additionally, Fig. S8 shows that the particle size of Pt in the Pt/30-CDs/Zr-MOF composite is approximately 2.84 nm, and these nanoparticles are more uniformly distributed compared to other samples. The HR-TEM image of Pt/30-CDs/Zr-MOF presented in Fig. 2 d indicates that the crystal distance of CDs is 0.25 nm, aligning well with the (100) crystal plane spacing of CDs, which suggests that the CDs are effectively encapsulated within the pores of the Zr-MOF (this phenomenon is also observable in Figs. S3-S6). Furthermore, the elemental distribution of the samples was assessed using energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) (Fig. 2 e), revealing a uniform distribution of the elements (C, Zr, Pt, and O) throughout the sample.

The crystal structures of the individual catalysts, Pt/x-CDs/Zr-MOF and 30-CDs/Zr-MOF, were analyzed using XRD, as illustrated in Fig. 3
a. The XRD plots reveal that the diffraction peaks of Pt/Zr-MOF and Pt/x-CDs/Zr-MOF (where x = 10, 20, 30, 50) remain consistent following the addition of CDs, indicating that the mild pyrolysis treatment of glucose did not alter the pristine crystal structure of Zr-MOF. However, the introduction of CDs resulted in a reduction of the intensity of the diffraction peaks for both Pt/x-CDs/Zr-MOF (x = 10, 20, 30, 50) and the 30-CDs/Zr-MOF catalysts, suggesting that the in-situ generated CDs compromised the crystallinity of the MOF [23]. Notably, the absence of discernible diffraction peaks corresponding to Pt crystals in the figure suggests that platinum is well-dispersed within the catalyst matrix [24], which is consistent with the EDS results presented in Fig. 2 e. To further investigate the specific surface area and porosity of the synthesized samples, N2 adsorption–desorption measurements were conducted, and the pore size distribution curves were derived using the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) method (Fig. 3 b and Fig. S9). The isotherms for all analyzed samples, as depicted in Fig. 3 b, exhibit I-shaped curves and an H4 hysteresis loop [25]. The BJH pore size distribution curves in Fig. S8 indicate that the majority of catalyst pore sizes are below 2 nm, and the sharp increase in N2 adsorption observed in the low-pressure region suggests a substantial presence of micropores, likely attributable to the inherent microporous structure of the MOF [17]. Table 1 summarized the BET-specific surface areas, average pore diameters, and pore volumes for Pt/x-CDs/Zr-MOF and 30-CDs/Zr-MOF. The specific surface areas of the Pt-loaded CDs/Zr-MOF samples varied between 743.71 and 903.66 m2 g−1, while the average pore diameters ranged from 1.85 to 1.96 nm. Notably, the data indicate that the specific surface area and pore volume of the samples containing CDs are significantly lower than those of the catalysts without CDs, accompanied by a slight reduction in pore size. These observations may be attributed to the obstruction of certain microporous channels following the incorporation of CDs into the MOFs [26]. With the small amounts of CDs added, the specific surface area increased first, and continued to increase the CDs load, the specific surface area decreased. This may be due to the increase in CDs causes the blocked part of the pores of MOFs, resulting in the reduction of the specific surface area of the catalyst [26,27].

The photoinduced electron change process of Pt/30-CDs/Zr-MOF was investigated using in-situ X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (in-situ XPS). The elemental types identified were consistent with the EDS analysis results presented in Fig. 2 e. As illustrated in Fig. 4
a, it can be seen that the C 1 s spectrum shows three different types of carbon bonding: C=C (284.8 eV), C-O (286.0 eV), and C=O (288.8 eV) [28,29]. And the binding energy of C-O is shifted to higher binding energies in the light conditions as compared to the without-light condition. Qin et al., demonstrated that strong binding between Zr-MOF and CDs enhances charge transfer due to electronic coupling at the interface [8]. Analysis of the in-situ XPS of Zr shows that the binding energies of Zr 3d 3/2 and Zr 3d 5/2 are 185.03 eV and 182.66 eV in the light, corresponding to 185.06 eV and 182.70 eV in the dark condition. The shift of their binding energies to lower binding energies in the light condition compared to the absence of light indicates an increase in the electron density (Fig. 4 b) [30]. As can be seen through Fig. 4 c, the in-situ XPS spectrum of O 1 s shows two prominent peaks indicating the presence of two different oxygen species. The peak at about 530 eV (labeled OL) corresponds to the lattice oxygen of zirconia, whereas the peak at about 532 eV (labeled Ov) is associated with oxygen adsorbed due to the presence of oxygen vacancies, and it was observed that the binding energy of OL and Ov shifted to a lower binding energy with increasing light, indicating an increase in the electron density [31]. As shown in Fig. 4 d, the in-situ XPS tests on the Pt and found that the binding energies of Pt 4f 7/2 and Pt 4f 5/2 were 70.65 eV and 73.37 eV in light, and the corresponding binding energies in dark conditions were 70.68 eV and 73.80 eV, respectively. The binding energies of Pt 4f shifted to lower binding energies under light conditions compared to without light illumination, indicating an increase in electron density. Therefore, the photo-generated electrons are adopted by the Pt and Zr sites, and the holes are captured by the C site of Pt/30-CDs/Zr-MOF. Notably, no significant changes were observed in the spectra of other elements in the catalyst after extended cycling compared to the fresh catalyst, indicating that the sample maintains good structural stability (Fig. S10).

Furthermore, Fig. 5
a shows the EPR results of Pt/30-CDs/Zr-MOF and Pt/Zr-MOF catalysts, and the result showed that Pt/30-CDs/Zr-MOF exhibits a stronger signal at g = 2.003 as compared to Pt/Zr-MOF catalyst. This result indicates that Pt/30-CDs/Zr-MOF contains abundant oxygen defects, which may provide rich active sites for CO2 adsorption and activation. Fig. 5 b presents the results of programmed temperature CO2 desorption. The data indicate that three samples exhibit pronounced CO2-TPD signals at weakly basic sites and moderately strong basic sites. The adsorption–desorption capacity of CO2 on the catalyst surface significantly influences the RWGS reaction. The desorption peak observed at approximately 158.8 °C corresponds to physical adsorption, and the peak around 250 °C corresponds to moderately basic sites. Whereas, the desorption peak observed at 390 °C may be attributed to defective sites, promoting CO2 adsorption and activation. It can be observed that between 100 °C and 350 °C, 30-CDs/Zr-MOF exhibits a strong CO2 adsorption capacity, and the CO2 adsorption capacity of Pt/30-CDs/Zr-MOF is stronger after 350 °C. By observing the peaks around 220 °C it was found that the desorption peaks were shifted to higher temperatures in Pt/30-CDs/Zr-MOF compared to Pt/Zr-MOF and 30-CDs/Zr-MOF. Fig. 5 c illustrates the reduction properties of the prepared samples and the interaction between Pt and the carriers, as investigated through H2-TPR, where the peak at 220 °C can be attributed to the reduction peak of PtO2. In comparison to Pt/Zr-MOF, the reduction peak of Pt oxide in Pt/30-CDs/Zr-MOF is shifted to higher temperatures, likely due to the strong interactions between the well-dispersed Pt oxide on the catalyst surface, following the encapsulation of CDs and the carriers [32]. As can be seen from Fig. 5 d, Pt/30-CDs/Zr-MOF possessed a relative weak CO-TPD signal, indicating that Pt/30-CDs/Zr-MOF has a weak adsorption ability of CO, resulting in good CO selectivity. In contrast, the catalyst Pt/Zr-MOF had a strong adsorption of CO, illustrating not favorable for the desorption on catalysts surface, which may cause the further occurrence of the catalytic process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal catalytic performance for CO2 reduction
ce:section.ce:para:
The performance of these catalysts in the RWGS reaction was evaluated in a quartz glass tube fixed-bed reactor. A gas mixture composed of CO2, H2, and N2 in a volume ratio of 1:4:2 was continuously introduced into the reactor at a flow rate of 64 mL min−1, ensuring a gas-hourly space velocity of 24000 mL gcat -1h−1. The synthesized catalysts were also tested for their pure thermal and photothermal CO2 reduction capabilities using a 300 W Xe lamp as the light source within a temperature range of 200 °C to 400 °C. The reaction products identified were CO and CH4, with CO being the predominant product; this predominance may be attributed to the highly dispersed Pt, which favors CO formation [33,34]. Fig. 6
a illustrates the CO2 conversion rates of the five catalysts under both pure thermal and photothermal conditions. Notably, the introduction of light significantly enhanced the CO2 conversion of the catalysts compared to the pure thermal condition, suggesting that light plays a beneficial role in CO2 conversion [34]. Furthermore, the catalyst containing both CDs and Pt exhibited a more pronounced increase in CO2 conversion compared to the catalyst with only Pt loading, indicating that the synergistic effect between Pt and CDs can substantially enhance the CO2 conversion in the RWGS reaction. Fig. 6 b illustrates that the selectivity of the catalysts Pt/x-CDs/Zr-MOF (where x = 10, 20) is approximately 80 % at 200 °C. Furthermore, the CO selectivity for all catalysts exceeds 99 % when the temperature is raised to 250 °C, likely due to the enhanced selectivity for CH4 at lower temperatures [35]. Fig. 6 c presents the CO yields at 300 °C under both pure thermal and photothermal conditions. Notably, the catalytic CO yield of Pt/30-CDs/Zr-MOF under pure thermal conditions at 300 °C was measured at 13.22 mmol gcat -1h−1, which increased to 22.52 mmol gcat -1h−1 with the introduction of light. The CO yields for all catalysts under photothermal conditions at 300 °C were approximately double those achieved under pure thermal conditions at the same temperature, reinforcing the conclusion that the incorporation of light significantly enhances the reaction rate. Additionally, the catalytic activity of the Pt/x-CDs/Zr-MOF samples with varying CDs concentrations exhibited a volcano curve variation (refer to Fig. S11 for the conversion rate variation). The catalytic CO yield increased with the rising CDs content, reaching an optimum performance at x = 30, before declining when x was increased to 50. This decline may be attributed to excessive CDs shielding the incident light and forming a carrier recombination center [8]，as further illustrated by the subsequent photovoltaic performance. To further investigate the exceptional catalytic performance of Pt/30-CDs/Zr-MOF, we conducted contact angle tests on the catalysts, as illustrated in Fig. S12. The contact angles of the catalysts after the encapsulation of CDs were consistently larger than those measured prior to encapsulation. Notably, the contact angle of Pt/30-CDs/Zr-MOF reached a maximum value of 72°, while the contact angle of the Pt/Zr-MOF catalyst was only 44°. This significant difference suggests that the Pt/Zr-MOF catalyst exhibits superior hydrophilicity, which may account for its comparatively lower catalytic performance. The apparent activation energy (Ea) was calculated using the Arrhenius equation to further investigate the differences in reaction rates under varying conditions (Fig. 6 d) [9]. The results indicate that the activation energy of Pt/Zr-MOF is greater than that of Pt/30-CDs/Zr-MOF, suggesting that the synergistic effect of Pt and CDs reduces the activation energy required for the reaction and facilitates the reduction of CO2. Notably, the Ea of Pt/30-CDs/Zr-MOF was approximately 70.00 kJ mol−1 under purely thermal conditions, which decreased to 59.49 kJ mol−1 under photothermal conditions. As illustrated in the figure, the Ea value for the photothermal CO2-catalyzed reaction is lower than that for the purely thermal-catalyzed reaction. Fig. S13a further demonstrates the activation energy variations for other catalysts, all of which indicate that light exposure can lower the activation energy of the reaction. Additionally, the stability of Pt/30-CDs/Zr-MOF was assessed at 300 °C with a light intensity of 1.2 W cm−2. Fig. S13b demonstrates the activation energy of Pt/30-CDs/Zr-MOF at different light intensities, and the result showed that the activation energy decreases as the light intensity increases, further illustrating the importance of light illumination. As shown in Fig. 6 e, after 16 h of cycling, the CO2 conversion under pure thermal conditions was maintained at approximately 7 %, while under photothermal conditions, the conversion increased to around 12 %. The selectivity remained consistent at about 99 % in both scenarios, showing no significant change in reaction performance compared to that of the fresh catalyst. After stability tests, TEM and SEM images revealed no significant aggregation of Pt NPs, with the particle size of Pt NPs measuring 3.42 nm after extensive cycling, a value that did not differ markedly from that observed prior to the reaction (Fig. 7 and Figs. S14). Furthermore, the spent catalysts of XRD analysis indicated negligible changes compared to pre-reaction conditions (Fig. S15), further corroborating the exceptional stability of the Pt/30-CDs/Zr-MOF catalyst. To further investigate the catalytic performance of Pt/30-CDs/Zr-MOF, we conducted tests at 300 °C, varying light intensities and gas-hourly space velocities (Fig. S16). The results clearly indicate that the catalyst's performance improves with increasing light intensity. Conversely, as the gas-hour speed velocity increases, CO2 conversion decreases while CO yield rises. Furthermore, we compared the performance of Pt/30-CDs/Zr-MOF in this study with that of recently reported catalysts for the RWGS reaction (Fig. 6 f, Fig. S17, and Table S2). Our findings demonstrate that Pt/30-CDs/Zr-MOF exhibits strong catalytic activity in CO2 reduction, outperforming many of the catalysts currently employed for the RWGS reaction.

Fig. 8
a presents the PL of each catalyst, aimed at evaluating the separation of photogenerated carriers and the migration process. Notably, 30-CDs/Zr-MOF demonstrates a higher photoluminescence intensity, indicative of significant photogenerated carrier recombination [25]. In contrast, the relatively low intensity observed in the catalyst after the addition of Pt suggests that the synergistic effect between CDs and Pt effectively mitigates the recombination of photogenerated carriers. The lowest peak intensity recorded for Pt/30-CDs/Zr-MOF confirms its superior efficiency in photogenerated carrier separation. Although the photoluminescence intensity of Pt/50-CDs/Zr-MOF is higher in comparison, this increase implies that an excess of CDs may act as a recombination center for carriers, potentially contributing to a decline in its catalytic performance [8]. Additionally, transient photocurrent measurements were employed to further assess the separation efficiency of the photogenerated carriers. As illustrated in Fig. 8 b, Pt/30-CDs/Zr-MOF exhibits a pronounced photocurrent response, signifying enhanced separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers [36]. Conversely, the Pt/50-CDs/Zr-MOF shows a slight decrease in response, which may be attributed to the excessive CDs either shielding a portion of the incident light or serving as a recombination center. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) Nyquist plot effectively identifies interfacial charge transfer during the reaction (Fig. 8 c). The minimum semicircle observed for Pt/30-CDs/Zr-MOF indicates a strong carrier transport capacity, further illustrating the high efficiency of photogenerated carrier separation in this material. In comparison, the EIS Nyquist plot for Pt/50-CDs/Zr-MOF suggests that it is more efficient than its counterpart. However, the slightly larger EIS Nyquist curve for the MOF indicates that an excess of CDs may compromise its carrier transport capacity, ultimately leading to a decrease in catalyst performance. Mott-Schottky (M−S) plots, which depict the flat band positions of each catalyst, are presented in Fig. 8 d. Both the Pt/x-CDs/Zr-MOF and 30-CDs/Zr-MOF samples exhibit positive slopes, confirming their classification as n-type semiconductors. To elucidate the energy band structure of the semiconductor materials, including conduction band, valence band, and forbidden band widths, we conducted UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) tests (Fig. S18). This analysis allowed us to characterize differences in the samples' light-absorbing abilities and to determine their corresponding forbidden band widths using the Tauc plot method [19,37]. Notably, the absorption spectra beyond 500 nm reveal that the Pt-loaded catalysts exhibit stronger absorption intensity, likely due to the interaction between Pt and CDs/Zr-MOF, which enhances light absorption. Furthermore, the Pt/30-CDs/Zr-MOF demonstrates a more pronounced absorption intensity, indicating superior light absorption properties and the capability to generate abundant photogenerated carriers under illumination [25,38]. The flat band potentials of the catalysts were determined as illustrated in Fig. 8 d, revealing that all samples exhibited positive slopes, characteristic of n-type semiconductors. In comparison to the flat band potential, the conduction band potential (ECB) of n-type semiconductors is approximately −0.1 V [39]. The ECB of Pt/10-CDs/Zr-MOF, Pt/20-CDs/Zr-MOF, Pt/30-CDs/Zr-MOF, Pt/50-CDs/Zr-MOF, Pt/Zr-MOF, and 30-CDs/Zr-MOF, were found to be −0.91 V, −0.60 V, −0.76 V, −0.65 V, −0.70 V, and −0.80 V, respectively. Additionally, the Tauc plot presented in Fig. 8 e indicates that the bandgap widths for these catalysts are 3.01 V, 3.15 V, 2.75 V, 2.88 V, 3.45 V, and 3.26 V. Based on the ECB and the bandgap widths, the effective bandgap energies of the catalysts are calculated to be 2.10 V, 2.55 V, 1.99 V, 2.23 V, 2.75 V, and 2.46 V for Pt/10-CDs/Zr-MOF, Pt/20-CDs/Zr-MOF, Pt/30-CDs/Zr-MOF, Pt/50-CDs/Zr-MOF, Pt/Zr-MOF, and 30-CDs/Zr-MOF, respectively. The energy band structures of the catalysts are depicted in Fig. 8 f. Notably, the bandgap width of Pt/30-CDs/Zr-MOF, measured at 2.75 V, is the narrowest among the six samples. A smaller bandgap facilitates the generation of photoexcited electron-hole pairs, thereby enhancing the efficiency of the photothermally catalyzed CO2 reduction reaction [19], which aligns with the observed reaction properties shown in Fig. 6 a.

Fig. 9 illustrates the infrared thermography of the Pt/x-CDs/Zr-MOF and 30-CDs/Zr-MOF catalysts when exposed to xenon lamps. The recorded surface temperatures for the catalysts were as follows: Pt/10-CDs/Zr-MOF at 50.5 °C, Pt/20-CDs/Zr-MOF at 54.5 °C, 30-CDs/Zr-MOF at 43.3 °C, Pt/30-CDs/Zr-MOF at 60 °C, Pt/50-CDs/Zr-MOF at 52.0 °C, and Pt/Zr-MOF at 42.2 °C. Notably, Pt/30-CDs/Zr-MOF exhibited the highest temperature among these six samples. Elevated photothermal temperatures suggest a greater potential for photothermal catalysis applications, enabling the efficient conversion of light energy into heat energy, thereby significantly enhancing the performance of the photothermal catalytic RWGS reaction [40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal catalytic mechanism for CO2 reduction
ce:section.ce:para:
To investigate the generation reasons and reaction pathways of the photothermal co-catalyzed RWGS reaction, the intermediates on Pt/30-CDs/Zr-MOF during the photothermal RWGS reaction were examined using in-situ DRIFTS. Pure CO2 gas was first passed into the gas as shown in Fig. S19 , where the 2316–2378 cm−1 corresponds to CO2. Subsequently, a CO2/H2 gas mixture was passed into the gas, as shown in Fig. 10
a, the peaks observed during the reaction were CO2 (2310–2375 and 3725 cm−1), CO3 2– (1796 cm−1), the linear *CO (2029 cm−1) and COOH* (1121 cm−1). COOH* is usually regarded as a key intermediate in the generation of CO, which suggests that the Pt/30-CDs/Zr-MOF photothermal co-catalyzed RWGS reaction follows the COOH* mechanism. In addition, the peak at linear CO justifies the product CO. Based on the various characterization results presented above, potential pathways for the CO2 conversion reaction are proposed, as illustrated in Fig. 10 b. Under full-spectrum irradiation, electrons are excited into the conduction band. The incorporation of CDs as local electron acceptors facilitates the transfer of electrons from the conduction band of Zr-MOF to the CDs, effectively enhancing the separation of photogenerated electrons and holes [41]. Moreover, CDs also serve as excellent electron donors, supplying additional electrons to participate in the reaction [42]. The incorporation of Pt NPs extends the visible light response range [43]. Light irradiation promotes the generation of hydrogen atoms (H*) at the Pt sites, providing H* at a significantly faster rate than thermal excitation. This, in conjunction with CO2 adsorbed on the carrier, may lead to the formation of COOH* intermediates, from which CO can dissociate following further activation of the C-O bond in COOH* [44], confirmed by COOH* at 1121 cm−1 in the in-situ DRIFTS. The smaller particle size of the Pt NPs (2.84 nm) in Pt/30-CDs/Zr-MOF offers a greater density of active sites for hydrogen dissociation, thereby accelerating the reaction and resulting in the highest CO2 conversion and CO production rate. Additionally, the enhanced CO2 reduction performance may also stem from the reduction of CO2 by photogenerated electrons, while the application of heat further accelerates the reaction and facilitates the desorption of the product CO [39]. Photothermal imaging results indicate that Pt/30-CDs/Zr-MOF exhibits superior photogenerated thermal phenomena, leading to increased surface temperatures of the catalyst, which in turn promotes the reaction [34,45]. Observation of Fig. 6 c indicates that the rate of CO generation gradually increases with rising glucose concentration. This phenomenon can be attributed to the strong interaction between Pt NPs and CDs/Zr-MOF, which reduces the recombination of photogenerated carriers and lowers the activation energy of the reaction, thereby promoting CO generation [11]. However, an excess of CDs may obstruct incident light and serve as a recombination center for carriers, leading to a decrease in CO yield.

In summary, we designed and synthesized a series of Pt/x-CDs/Zr-MOF and 30-CDs/Zr-MOF catalysts, wherein Zr-MOF was encapsulated with varying amounts of CDs to facilitate photothermal catalytic RWGS reactions at low-temperatures, aimed at the selective production of CO. The resulting catalysts exhibited abundant oxygen defects and favorable photothermal properties. The CDs/Zr-MOF was produced by converting glucose into ultra-small-sized CDs that were encapsulated within Zr-MOF through mild pyrolysis. The synergistic interaction between Pt and CDs contributed to a broad spectrum of solar absorption and minimized the recombination of photogenerated carriers. The Pt/30-CDs/Zr-MOF samples demonstrated remarkable performance in low-temperature RWGS, achieving a CO yield of 13.22 mmol gcat -1h−1 under pure thermal conditions at 300 °C, with a selectivity exceeding 99 %. Upon the introduction of light at the same reaction temperature, the CO yield increased to 22.52 mmol gcat -1h−1, nearly doubling the yield obtained under purely thermal conditions. Importantly, the combination of light and the synergistic effects of Pt and CDs significantly lowered the activation energy of the reaction. Consequently, the exceptional photothermal effect of Pt/30-CDs/Zr-MOF substantially enhances the performance of the RWGS reaction. This study presents the development of photothermal co-catalysts for the effective reduction of CO2 via the RWGS reaction, providing new insights into the application of precious metal-loaded MOF-encapsulated CDs in the realm of photothermal catalysis for RWGS.
